news_list.title: Vietnam under pressure to scale up battery storage to support renewable growth
news_list.summary:
Hanoi (VNS/VNA) - Vietnam is coming under growing pressure to make battery energy storage systems (BESS) a cornerstone of its power infrastructure, as rapid renewable expansion and rising electricity demand, driven by double-digit economic growth, expose mounting strain on the national grid.

The global energy landscape is shifting quickly. The transition to green energy is moving into a new phase, from rapid expansion of renewable generation towards strengthening storage capacity to ensure system flexibility and grid reliability.

In Vietnam, renewable energy has expanded rapidly in recent years, laying the foundation for the country's green transition but without sufficient storage capacity, renewable power cannot be fully utilised to support long-term economic growth.

Analysts said the next phase of the energy transition will depend less on how much renewable power is added and more on how effectively it can be stored and dispatched.

"BESS should be seen as essential infrastructure, not just a supporting technology," said Phan Cong Tien, Director of the Institute of Smart Energy Application Research (iSEAR). "Without storage, it will be difficult to increase the share of renewables while maintaining system stability."

Market shortfall

Vietnam's energy storage sector could reach about 6 billion USD by 2030, reflecting national energy development goals under the Politburo's Resolution 70-NQ/TW on ensuring energy security to 2030 with a vision to 2045 and the revised eighth Power Development Plan (PDP8).

However, current deployment remains limited. As of early 2025, installed BESS capacity is estimated at under 100 MW, far below the PDP8 target of 10,000 to 16,300 MW by 2030.

The shortfall highlights a key challenge: without a rapid scale-up in storage, the broader energy transition will struggle to deliver.

According to Deputy Director General of the National Electricity System and Market Operation Company Limited (NSMO) Nguyen Quoc Trung, electricity demand in Vietnam is expected to continue rising sharply in the coming years, driven by double-digit growth targets, digital transformation and a rapid electrification trend.

Amid volatile global energy markets placing significant pressure on the need for a stable, secure and sustainable power supply, rooftop solar combined with storage is emerging as a key solution to stabilise the system. BESS can help improve the system's ability to absorb renewable energy and enhance operational flexibility.

The Prime Minister's Directive No. 10/CT-TTg dated March 30 signals a policy shift from simply ensuring sufficient power supply to easing system pressure through demand-side management and distributed energy solutions.

The directive places electricity savings alongside load management, demand shifting and the development of on-site generation, creating new scope for energy storage solutions such as BESS to play a more central role in the power system.

The market is already gaining an early foothold as companies position themselves to capitalise on BESS potential, with participation from international players such as Goldwind and Schneider Electric alongside domestic firms including GG Industries and VinFast Energy.

GG Industries is set to launch a 5 GWh-per-year BESS plant in Hung Yen. Vingroup has also identified energy and infrastructure as strategic pillars, signalling growing corporate interest in the sector while Viettel Construction is expanding into BESS deployment to complement its renewable energy portfolio.

Policy bottlenecks

Despite growing interest, several barriers continue to limit deployment.

High upfront costs and limited access to green finance remained key constraints on investment, said Nguyen Anh Tuan, Vice President of the Vietnam Energy Association, citing financial and infrastructure risks in projects linked to direct power purchase agreements (DPPA).

In addition, the absence of a clear regulatory framework is hindering the deployment of BESS.

BESS is not yet fully defined within the power system and there is no dedicated market for ancillary services such as frequency regulation or voltage support, making it difficult for investors to secure stable revenue streams, according to Nguyen Van Thien, deputy chief executive for business and market development at GG Power Industry.

Thien stressed the need to clarify the role of BESS in the power system through the introduction of competitive procurement mechanisms and pricing structures that reflect time-of-use patterns and system costs.

"Storage is not just about equipment. It must be integrated into the overall energy system," Thiện said, noting that clearer regulations, pricing mechanisms and technical standards are needed to accelerate deployment.

Path forward

Experts said a comprehensive policy framework will be key to unlocking the sector, including competitive procurement mechanisms, time-of-use pricing structures and stronger integration of BESS into direct power purchase agreements (DPPA) and self-consumption models.

According to Tien, policy reforms should begin with recognising BESS as a distinct asset class, followed by the development of market mechanisms that reflect its multiple functions, including peak shaving, load shifting and grid support.

Financial incentives such as tax breaks, concessional loans and improved access to green finance are also needed to reduce investment risks.

In addition, greater attention must be given to strengthening technical standards and safety regulations.

As BESS deployment scales up, issues such as fire safety, system integration and end-of-life battery management were becoming increasingly important, said Nguyen Truong Phuc Khanh, Deputy Director of the Ho Chi Minh City Office of Long Tech Trading & Technical Co., Ltd.

"Aligning with international standards while developing domestic guidelines will be key to ensuring safe and sustainable growth," he said.

Tran Thuy Tien, development director at Constant Energy, said safety rather than financial returns remained the top priority in deploying BESS projects.

She said this would include both technical safety, such as compliant system design, proper installation, stable operation and fire and electrical protection, as well as legal safety, ensuring full adherence to Vietnam's regulations, power planning requirements and transparent investment procedures.

Clearer policies on rooftop solar, direct power purchase agreements (DPPA) and energy storage were needed to create a stable legal framework for project implementation, she stressed.

"Demand is already there and growth potential is clear," said economist Dinh Trong Thinh. "What is needed now is a clear development pathway, including technology strategy and policy support.

"With demand for energy storage already emerging and growth potential clear and domestic manufacturing capacity yet to take shape, the gap presents an opportunity that could translate into significant room for BESS industry development."

He stressed that developing the sector would require a clear roadmap focused on mastering core technologies and building domestic capabilities alongside stronger linkages across the value chain./.

VNA

news_list.title: PM Modi calls NITI Aayog a 'vital pillar' of Indian political architecture - ExBulletin
news_list.summary:
New Delhi [India]April 25 (ANI): Prime Minister Narendra Modi highlighted the importance of the NITI Aayog in India's governance framework, describing it as a crucial driver of reforms, innovation and cooperative federalism.

Highlighting the role of the policy think tank, the Prime Minister said NITI Aayog has contributed significantly in strengthening policy making and improving the comfort of living across the country. The Prime Minister also extended his wishes to the other newly appointed members of the reconstituted body.

"NITI Aayog has become a vital pillar of India's political architecture, fostering cooperative federalism, fostering reforms and enhancing 'ease of living'. It serves as a dynamic platform for innovation and long-term strategic thinking across all sectors," Prime Minister Modi said on X.

"The Government has reconstituted the NITI Aayog. My best wishes to Shri Ashok Kumar Lahiri Ji to become Vice-Chairman. My best wishes also to Shri Rajiv Gauba Ji, Prof. KV Raju Ji, Prof. Gobardhan Das Ji, Prof. Abhay Karandikar Ji and Dr. M. Srinivas Ji to become full-time members of the NITI Aayog. I wish them all a productive and impactful term ahead," he said added.

In another article

In an article on His rich experience in economics and public policy will significantly strengthen India's reform journey and the journey towards becoming a Viksit Bharat. I am confident that his efforts will further energize policymaking in our country. My best wishes for a successful mandate.

Lahiri, who was appointed vice-chairman of the government's apex policy think tank on Friday, held a meeting with Prime Minister Narendra Modi in New Delhi, marking the start of his tenure at a time when India is navigating a complex global economic environment.

Although the details of the meeting have not been officially disclosed, the interaction assumes particular significance as NITI Aayog plays a key role in shaping India's economic policy framework and driving reforms across sectors.

The new Niti Aayog team includes two eminent experts from West Bengal: senior economist Dr Ashok Lahiri as vice-chairman and scientist Dr Gobardhan Das as member, sources said on Friday. (ANI)

news_list.title: India's strategic autonomy in a multipolar world - ExBulletin
news_list.summary:
Strategic autonomy is not just a slogan. It's a strategy. It's the art of navigating a turbulent world without losing your bearings. As the global order evolves, India must continue to walk a tightrope: engaging with the United States without becoming a vassal, deterring China without provoking war, and partnering with Russia without inheriting its isolation. It must invest in capabilities, cultivate partnerships and assert its interests with clarity and confidence.

Introduction

In the lexicon of international relations, few concepts have evolved as dynamically as "strategic autonomy." Once reserved for academic debate, it now sits at the heart of India's foreign policy discourse, shaping decisions in an increasingly multipolar and unstable world.

As global power shifts accelerate and traditional alliances fray, India finds itself on a delicate path between competing giants: the United States, China and Russia. The pursuit of strategic autonomy is no longer a theoretical aspiration. This is a daily diplomatic practice, fraught with complexity and consequences.

Strategic autonomy refers to a nation's ability to make sovereign foreign policy and defense decisions without being constrained by external pressures or alliance obligations. This is not synonymous with isolationism or neutrality. Rather, it implies flexibility, independence and the ability to engage with multiple powers on its own terms.

For India, the concept has deep historical roots, dating back to colonial subjugation and Free India's determination never to let anyone else decide for us our place in the world. From Nehru's non-alignment during the Cold War to the Modi government's "multi-alignment" in the current era, successive governments have sought to preserve India's freedom of action while adapting to changing geopolitical realities.

In theory, strategic autonomy offers a middle path between rigid bloc politics and passive disengagement. In practice, this requires skillful diplomacy, institutional resilience and a clear-eyed understanding of the national interest. It is a balancing act - sometimes precarious, often imperfect, but essential for a country like India that aspires to global leadership without becoming a client state.

Paths and obstacles for India

The current global landscape presents both opportunities and challenges for India's strategic autonomy. The unipolar moment of American dominance has given way to a fragmented world order, in which China's assertiveness, Russia's revisionism, and the West's internal divisions, accentuated by Washington's unpredictability, create a fluid and unpredictable environment.

For India, this means recalibrating its relations with major powers while preserving its fundamental interests: territorial integrity, economic growth, technological progress and regional stability.

Relations between India and the United States have deepened significantly over the past two decades. From defense cooperation and intelligence sharing to joint military exercises and technology transfers, the strategic partnership has matured. The Quad grouping (Australia, Japan, India and the United States), the Indo-Pacific dialogues, the newly formed I2U2 (India, Israel, the United Arab Emirates and the United States) and the India-Middle East-Europe Economic Corridor (IMEEC) and shared concerns over the rise of China have further strengthened ties.

Yet the relationship is not without friction. The Trump administration's erratic trade policies, followed by drastic tariffs and sanctions during its second term, have strained economic ties. Washington's pressure on India to scale back its energy and defense ties with Russia and align more closely with Western positions has tested New Delhi's resolve.

India's response was measured. It continues to engage with the United States, maintains independent positions on global conflicts, and emphasizes the primacy of national interest over ideological alignment. This is strategic autonomy in action, not anti-Americanism, but a refusal to be overwhelmed by American priorities.

An Indian delegation led by Prime Minister Narendra Modi and a Chinese delegation led by President Xi Jinping meet on the sidelines of the BRICS summit in Kazan, Russia on October 23, 2024. | @NarendraModi/X.

China presents a more complex challenge. Border clashes with India in 2020 shattered illusions of benign coexistence, and tensions remain high despite diplomatic overtures. Yet China is also one of India's largest trading partners, a key player in regional institutions, and a competitor whose actions shape the strategic environment. India's approach is one of cautious engagement and strong deterrence.

It strengthens border infrastructure, deepens ties with Indo-Pacific partners and invests in local defense capabilities. At the same time, it participates in multilateral forums such as BRICS and the Shanghai Cooperation Organization, where China plays a leading role - a difficult but necessary balancing act.

Strategic autonomy here means resisting both confrontation and capitulation. This means asserting sovereignty and refusing to be another country's counterweight to a rising China, while controlling China's access to the Indian economy and simultaneously keeping the channels of communication open. This means recognizing that rivalry does not preclude diplomacy and that decoupling is not always desirable or feasible.

India's relationship with Russia is rooted in Cold War solidarity, defense cooperation and shared strategic interests. Despite Moscow's growing proximity to Beijing and Russia's isolation following the Ukraine conflict, India has maintained its ties - buying oil, importing arms and engaging diplomatically. This drew criticism from Western capitals, but India held firm.

Its relationship with Russia is historic, multidimensional and not subject to external veto. It continues to diversify its military imports, invest in local production and explore new partnerships, but without abandoning old ones. Strategic autonomy here means refusing to choose sides in a binary competition and, instead, crafting a foreign policy that reflects India's unique geography, history and aspirations.

During India's G20 presidency in 2023, Prime Minister Narendra Modi declared that India was now the voice of the South - undefeated, plural and powerful. Its democracy, he says, is not just a system but a "bouquet of hope", nourished by the strength of its multilingual and multicultural fabric. Foreign Minister S. Jaishankar says partnerships must be shaped by interest and not by sentiments or inherited biases.

This is diplomacy with a backbone - assertive, pragmatic and decidedly Indian, seeking to be "non-Western" without being "anti-Western".

This position resonates in the Global South, where many emerging and middle powers concerned about peace and stability in their own regions choose to safeguard their geopolitical and economic interests rather than get drawn into the vortex of great power rivalries. They seek action, not alignment; voice, no vassalage.

What emerges is a vision of India as a sovereign hub in a rebalancing world - a nation that neither blindly aligns nor isolates itself. The rise of India is therefore civilizational, plural and deeply political. Although India's quest for strategic autonomy is principled, it faces headwinds as it walks a tightrope among major powers. The global economy is increasingly interdependent and technology ecosystems are dominated by a few players. Defense modernization requires partnerships and climate diplomacy requires coordination. In such a world, autonomy must be redefined - not as isolation, but as resilience and adaptability.

Domestic factors also play a role. Political polarization, economic vulnerabilities, and institutional constraints can limit the effectiveness of autonomous decision-making. Strategic autonomy requires not only diplomatic skills, but also economic strength, technological capabilities and political coherence. We cannot be truly self-reliant from a position of weakness.

Moreover, in a world of cyber threats, artificial intelligence warfare, and space competition, autonomy must extend beyond traditional domains. It must encompass data sovereignty, digital infrastructure and supply chain security. India's recent efforts to create indigenous platforms, secure critical minerals and assert its voice in global technology governance point in this direction.

More than a slogan

Strategic autonomy is not just a slogan. It's a strategy. It's the art of navigating a turbulent world without losing your bearings. As the global order evolves, India must continue to walk a tightrope: engaging with the United States without becoming a vassal, deterring China without provoking war, and partnering with Russia without inheriting its isolation. It must invest in capabilities, cultivate partnerships and assert its interests with clarity and confidence.

In doing so, India does not reject the world: it reclaims its action within it. Strategic autonomy is not about standing alone; it's about standing straight and upright.

news_list.title: MD SIDCO interacts with Samba Industrial Association
news_list.summary:
JAMMU, Apr 25: In a significant step towards strengthening the industry collaboration and to make the awareness for safeguarding the environment and natural resources especially in the Industrial Estate managed by J&K SIDCO, Managing Director, Pardeep Singh Manhas, held a meeting with the Industrial Associations and Industrial Unit holders of IGC, Samba.

During the meeting the concern regarding running of the Common Effluent Treatment Plant (CETP) constructed in IGC, Samba was emphasized and it was impressed that the running of the Common Effluent Treatment Plants (CETP) by SIDCO through Industrial Associations/Industrial units as users of the facility is of utmost importance.

Managing Director emphasized the stakeholders for signing a Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) between Industrial Associations/ Industrial units with SIDCO for the successful running of the CETP for the compliance of PCB guidelines from time to time and for the compliance of directions of Supreme Court of India for the particular CETP.

During the visit Managing Director also inspected the developmental works under execution at Industrial Estate of IGC, Samba and directed the engineering wing to complete the work in prescribed time.

The other officers who accompanied the Managing Director, were Superintendent Engineer (Civil), General Manager (A&P), General Manager (Jammu), Executive Engineer, (Civil) and Manager (Finance/A&P).
news_list.source: Jammu Kashmir Latest News | Tourism | Breaking News J&K

themes.name: 越南能源轉型與儲能需求
themes.insight: 越南正因再生能源快速擴張與電力需求增加而面臨供電壓力，亟需擴大規模建置電池儲能系統 (BESS) 以支持能源轉型並確保電網穩定性，但面臨資金、法規及技術標準的挑戰。
evidence: Vietnam under pressure to scale up battery storage to support renewable growth

Hanoi (VNS/VNA) - Vietnam is coming under growing pressure to make battery energy storage systems (BESS) a cornerstone of its power infrastructure, as rapid renewable expansion and rising electricity demand, driven by double-digit economic growth, expose mounting strain on the national grid.

High upfront costs and limited access to green finance remained key constraints on investment, said Nguyen Anh Tuan, Vice President of the Vietnam Energy Association, citing financial and infrastructure risks in projects linked to direct power purchase agreements (DPPA).
In addition, the absence of a clear regulatory framework is hindering the deployment of BESS.

themes.name: 印度戰略自主與多邊關係
themes.insight: 面對複雜多變的國際局勢，印度正積極推動戰略自主，在與美國深化合作、應對中國挑戰、維持與俄羅斯關係的同時，尋求自身利益最大化，展現其獨立自主的外交策略。
evidence: India's strategic autonomy in a multipolar world
Strategic autonomy refers to a nation's ability to make sovereign foreign policy and defense decisions without being constrained by external pressures or alliance obligations.
For India, the concept has deep historical roots, dating back to colonial subjugation and Free India's determination never to let anyone else decide for us our place in the world.
The current global landscape presents both opportunities and challenges for India's strategic autonomy.
Relations between India and the United States have deepened significantly over the past two decades.
China presents a more complex challenge.
India's relationship with Russia is rooted in Cold War solidarity, defense cooperation and shared strategic interests.

themes.name: 印度 NITI Aayog 改革與發展
themes.insight: 印度總理莫迪強調 NITI Aayog 作為國家政策智庫的重要性，視其為改革、創新與合作聯邦制的關鍵推動力，並對新任成員表達期望，旨在提升政策制定與國民生活便利性。

items.title: Vietnam under pressure to scale up battery storage to support renewable growth
items.summary:

items.title: India's strategic autonomy in a multipolar world - ExBulletin
items.summary:
Strategic autonomy is not just a slogan. It's a strategy. It's the art of navigating a turbulent world without losing your bearings. As the global order evolves, India must continue to walk a tightrope: engaging with the United States without becoming a vassal, deterring China without provoking war, and partnering with Russia without inheriting its isolation. Introduction

In the lexicon of international relations, few concepts have evolved as dynamically as "strategic autonomy." Once reserved for academic debate, it now sits at the heart of India's foreign policy discourse, shaping decisions in an increasingly multipolar and unstable world.

The pursuit of strategic autonomy is no longer a theoretical aspiration. This is a daily diplomatic practice, fraught with complexity and consequences.

Strategic autonomy refers to a nation's ability to make sovereign foreign policy and defense decisions without being constrained by external pressures or alliance obligations. This is not synonymous with isolationism or neutrality. Rather, it implies flexibility, independence and the ability to engage with multiple powers on its own terms.

For India, the concept has deep historical roots, dating back to colonial subjugation and Free India's determination never to let anyone else decide for us our place in the world. From Nehru's non-alignment during the Cold War to the Modi government's "multi-alignment" in the current era, successive governments have sought to preserve India's freedom of action while adapting to changing geopolitical realities.

In theory, strategic autonomy offers a middle path between rigid bloc politics and passive disengagement. In practice, this requires skillful diplomacy, institutional resilience and a clear-eyed understanding of the national interest. It is a balancing act - sometimes precarious, often imperfect, but essential for a country like India that aspires to global leadership without becoming a client state.

Paths and obstacles for India

The current global landscape presents both opportunities and challenges for India's strategic autonomy. The unipolar moment of American dominance has given way to a fragmented world order, in which China's assertiveness, Russia's revisionism, and the West's internal divisions, accentuated by Washington's unpredictability, create a fluid and unpredictable environment.

For India, this means recalibrating its relations with major powers while preserving its fundamental interests: territorial integrity, economic growth, technological progress and regional stability.

Relations between India and the United States have deepened significantly over the past two decades. From defense cooperation and intelligence sharing to joint military exercises and technology transfers, the strategic partnership has matured. The Quad grouping (Australia, Japan, India and the United States), the Indo-Pacific dialogues, the newly formed I2U2 (India, Israel, the United Arab Emirates and the United States) and the India-Middle East-Europe Economic Corridor (IMEEC) and shared concerns over the rise of China have further strengthened ties.

Yet the relationship is not without friction. The Trump administration's erratic trade policies, followed by drastic tariffs and sanctions during its second term, have strained economic ties. Washington's pressure on India to scale back its energy and defense ties with Russia and align more closely with Western positions has tested New Delhi's resolve.

India's response was measured. It continues to engage with the United States, maintains independent positions on global conflicts, and emphasizes the primacy of national interest over ideological alignment. This is strategic autonomy in action, not anti-Americanism, but a refusal to be overwhelmed by American priorities.

An Indian delegation led by Prime Minister Narendra Modi and a Chinese delegation led by President Xi Jinping meet on the sidelines of the BRICS summit in Kazan, Russia on October 23, 2024. | @NarendraModi/X.

China presents a more complex challenge. Border clashes with India in 2020 shattered illusions of benign coexistence, and tensions remain high despite diplomatic overtures. Yet China is also one of India's largest trading partners, a key player in regional institutions, and a competitor whose actions shape the strategic environment. India's approach is one of cautious engagement and strong deterrence.

It strengthens border infrastructure, deepens ties with Indo-Pacific partners and invests in local defense capabilities. At the same time, it participates in multilateral forums such as BRICS and the Shanghai Cooperation Organization, where China plays a leading role - a difficult but necessary balancing act.

Strategic autonomy here means resisting both confrontation and capitulation. This means asserting sovereignty and refusing to be another country's counterweight to a rising China, while controlling China's access to the Indian economy and simultaneously keeping the channels of communication open. This means recognizing that rivalry does not preclude diplomacy and that decoupling is not always desirable or feasible.

India's relationship with Russia is rooted in Cold War solidarity, defense cooperation and shared strategic interests. Despite Moscow's growing proximity to Beijing and Russia's isolation following the Ukraine conflict, India has maintained its ties - buying oil, importing arms and engaging diplomatically. This drew criticism from Western capitals, but India held firm.

Its relationship with Russia is historic, multidimensional and not subject to external veto. It continues to diversify its military imports, invest in local production and explore new partnerships, but without abandoning old ones. Strategic autonomy here means refusing to choose sides in a binary competition and, instead, crafting a foreign policy that reflects India's unique geography, history and aspirations.

During India's G20 presidency in 2023, Prime Minister Narendra Modi declared that India was now the voice of the South - undefeated, plural and powerful. Its democracy, he says, is not just a system but a "bouquet of hope", nourished by the strength of its multilingual and multicultural fabric. Foreign Minister S. Jaishankar says partnerships must be shaped by interest and not by sentiments or inherited biases.

This is diplomacy with a backbone - assertive, pragmatic and decidedly Indian, seeking to be "non-Western" without being "anti-Western".

This position resonates in the Global South, where many emerging and middle powers concerned about peace and stability in their own regions choose to safeguard their geopolitical and economic interests rather than get drawn into the vortex of great power rivalries. They seek action, not alignment; voice, no vassalage.

What emerges is a vision of India as a sovereign hub in a rebalancing world - a nation that neither blindly aligns nor isolates itself. The rise of India is therefore civilizational, plural and deeply political. Although India's quest for strategic autonomy is principled, it faces headwinds as it walks a tightrope among major powers. The global economy is increasingly interdependent and technology ecosystems are dominated by a few players. Defense modernization requires partnerships and climate diplomacy requires coordination. In such a world, autonomy must be redefined - not as isolation, but as resilience and adaptability.

Domestic factors also play a role. Political polarization, economic vulnerabilities, and institutional constraints can limit the effectiveness of autonomous decision-making. Strategic autonomy requires not only diplomatic skills, but also economic strength, technological capabilities and political coherence. We cannot be truly self-reliant from a position of weakness.

Moreover, in a world of cyber threats, artificial intelligence warfare, and space competition, autonomy must extend beyond traditional domains. It must encompass data sovereignty, digital infrastructure and supply chain security. India's recent efforts to create indigenous platforms, secure critical minerals and assert its voice in global technology governance point in this direction.

More than a slogan

Strategic autonomy is not just a slogan. It's a strategy. It's the art of navigating a turbulent world without losing your bearings. As the global order evolves, India must continue to walk a tightrope: engaging with the United States without becoming a vassal, deterring China without provoking war, and partnering with Russia without inheriting its isolation. It must invest in capabilities, cultivate partnerships and assert its interests with clarity and confidence.

In doing so, India does not reject the world: it reclaims its action within it. Strategic autonomy is not about standing alone; it's about standing straight and upright.

items.title: MD SIDCO interacts with Samba Industrial Association
items.summary:
JAMMU, Apr 25: In a significant step towards strengthening the industry collaboration and to make the awareness for safeguarding the environment and natural resources especially in the Industrial Estate managed by J&K SIDCO, Managing Director, Pardeep Singh Manhas, held a meeting with the Industrial Associations and Industrial Unit holders of IGC, Samba.

During the meeting the concern regarding running of the Common Effluent Treatment Plant (CETP) constructed in IGC, Samba was emphasized and it was impressed that the running of the Common Effluent Treatment Plants (CETP) by SIDCO through Industrial Associations/Industrial units as users of the facility is of utmost importance.

Managing Director emphasized the stakeholders for signing a Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) between Industrial Associations/ Industrial units with SIDCO for the successful running of the CETP for the compliance of PCB guidelines from time to time and for the compliance of directions of Supreme Court of India for the particular CETP.

During the visit Managing Director also inspected the developmental works under execution at Industrial Estate of IGC, Samba and directed the engineering wing to complete the work in prescribed time.

The other officers who accompanied the Managing Director, were Superintendent Engineer (Civil), General Manager (A&P), General Manager (Jammu), Executive Engineer, (Civil) and Manager (Finance/A&P).
items.source: Jammu Kashmir Latest News | Tourism | Breaking News J&K

topics.topic: 越南能源轉型與儲能需求
topics.summary: 越南正因再生能源快速擴張與電力需求增加而面臨供電壓力，亟需擴大規模建置電池儲能系統 (BESS) 以支持能源轉型並確保電網穩定性，但面臨資金、法規及技術標準的挑戰。
evidence: Vietnam under pressure to scale up battery storage to support renewable growth

Hanoi (VNS/VNA) - Vietnam is coming under growing pressure to make battery energy storage systems (BESS) a cornerstone of its power infrastructure, as rapid renewable expansion and rising electricity demand, driven by double-digit economic growth, expose mounting strain on the national grid.
The global energy landscape is shifting quickly.

In Vietnam, renewable energy has expanded rapidly in recent years, laying the foundation for the country's green transition but without sufficient storage capacity, renewable power cannot be fully utilised to support long-term economic growth.

"BESS should be seen as essential infrastructure, not just a supporting technology," said Phan Cong Tien, Director of the Institute of Smart Energy Application Research (iSEAR). "Without storage, it will be difficult to increase the share of renewables while maintaining system stability."

High upfront costs and limited access to green finance remained key constraints on investment, said Nguyen Anh Tuan, Vice President of the Vietnam Energy Association, citing financial and infrastructure risks in projects linked to direct power purchase agreements (DPPA).
In addition, the absence of a clear regulatory framework is hindering the deployment of BESS.

evidence: India's strategic autonomy in a multipolar world
Strategic autonomy refers to a nation's ability to make sovereign foreign policy and defense decisions without being constrained by external pressures or alliance obligations.
For India, the concept has deep historical roots, dating back to colonial subjugation and Free India's determination never to let anyone else decide for us our place in the world.
The current global landscape presents both opportunities and challenges for India's strategic autonomy.
Relations between India and the United States have deepened significantly over the past two decades.
China presents a more complex challenge.
India's relationship with Russia is rooted in Cold War solidarity, defense cooperation and shared strategic interests.

ai_analysis.full_text:
2026年4月26日 週末晨報
【今日結論】
今日台股市場預計將受到台積電 ADR 大漲的提振，開盤有望呈現上攻格局。然而，夜盤期指拉回以及地緣政治和原油價格的持續走高，為市場帶來潛在的隱憂。整體而言，市場將在科技股的拉抬與宏觀風險之間尋求平衡，投資人需密切關注盤面變化。
【新聞驅動】
AI 領域的動態依然是市場焦點，Google 大舉投資 Anthropic 展現了科技巨頭在算力與生態系佈局的決心，同時中國 DeepSeek 轉用華為晶片的舉動，預示著 AI 算力去美化趨勢的加速。費城半導體指數的強勁表現雖創紀錄，但 RSI 與乖離率已達極端，潛在回檔風險不容忽視。此外，中東局勢緊張推升布蘭特原油突破 105 美元，這不僅加劇了通膨壓力，更可能引發「需求破壞」，對全球經濟成長構成實質性威脅。
【國際與台股】
週五美股收紅，Nasdaq 和費半表現尤為亮眼，主要受惠於台積電 ADR 的強勁上漲。台積電 ADR 大漲 5.17%，換算至現貨價預示著台股開盤的潛力。然而，台指期夜盤下跌 1.8%，富台指持平，顯示週末市場的觀望情緒。日經 225 指數上漲 0.97%，KOSPI 則小幅持平，東北亞市場看法分歧。CNN 恐懼貪婪指數為 66 (貪婪)，與加密貨幣市場的 31 (恐懼) 背離，市場情緒複雜。
【KOL與情緒】
雖然目前數據中未直接提及 KOL 的評論，但從社群脈動的數據遺失 (social_pulse) 來看，市場對大眾情緒的追蹤能力暫時受阻，這意味著單純依賴社群情緒進行即時判斷存在盲點。投資人應更側重於基本面和技術面的分析，謹慎解讀市場訊號。
【操作含意】
下週一的關鍵在於台積電 ADR 帶來的開盤力道能否有效承接，並觀察夜盤的拉回是否被市場消化。原油價格突破關鍵價位是當前最主要的宏觀壓力源，投資人應謹慎評估其對消費及經濟成長的潛在衝擊。在多頭趨勢 (BULL) 和高波動性 (HIGH) 的市場環境下，應關注個股表現，並對風險保持高度警惕。

selected_charts.title: 美股與風險資產
selected_charts.description: S&P 500、Nasdaq、SOX、VIX 與美元、黃金、原油的跨資產訊號。
selected_charts.takeaway: 台積電 ADR 大漲 5.17% 提振市場信心，Nasdaq 期貨也上漲 1.86%，預示著科技股的強勁表現。然而，VIX 下跌 3.11% 顯示避險情緒有所減弱，需關注市場能否持續上攻。
selected_charts.action: 觀察後續科技股的領漲動能及市場情緒變化。

selected_charts.title: 科技主題熱度圖
selected_charts.description: 科技新聞主題與市場主線的熱度排名與來源證據。
selected_charts.takeaway: AI 產業前景樂觀依然是市場焦點，然而，中東局勢與美元強勢的風險因子不容忽視，這可能對科技股的估值造成壓力。
selected_charts.action: 關注 AI 相關新聞的發展，並警惕宏觀風險對科技股的影響。

selected_charts.title: 商品風險走勢
selected_charts.description: 布蘭特原油、黃金與美元指數的通膨與避險訊號。
selected_charts.takeaway: 布蘭特原油小幅上漲 0.25%，黃金上漲 0.37%，美元指數則下跌 0.29%。中東局勢的持續緊張可能支撐油價，而美元走弱則對黃金有利。
selected_charts.action: 關注地緣政治風險對能源與貴金屬市場的影響。

selected_charts.title: 美股分析師共識圖
selected_charts.description: Seeking Alpha 的 Quant、作者與華爾街評級共識，搭配多頭/空頭強弱排序。
selected_charts.takeaway: Seeking Alpha 的分析師共識顯示，Mag 7 股票中，TSLA 的共識評級為 3.18，GOOGL 的共識評級為 4.03，MSFT 為 4.03，這些科技巨頭的分析師評級較為樂觀。
selected_charts.action: 關注分析師對科技龍頭的看法，並與市場實際表現進行比對。

chart_commentary.takeaway: 台積電 ADR 大漲 5.17% 提振市場信心，Nasdaq 期貨也上漲 1.86%，預示著科技股的強勁表現。然而，VIX 下跌 3.11% 顯示避險情緒有所減弱，需關注市場能否持續上攻。
chart_commentary.action: 觀察後續科技股的領漲動能及市場情緒變化。

chart_commentary.takeaway: AI 產業前景樂觀依然是市場焦點，然而，中東局勢與美元強勢的風險因子不容忽視，這可能對科技股的估值造成壓力。
chart_commentary.action: 關注 AI 相關新聞的發展，並警惕宏觀風險對科技股的影響。

chart_commentary.takeaway: 布蘭特原油小幅上漲 0.25%，黃金上漲 0.37%，美元指數則下跌 0.29%。中東局勢的持續緊張可能支撐油價，而美元走弱則對黃金有利。
chart_commentary.action: 關注地緣政治風險對能源與貴金屬市場的影響。

chart_commentary.takeaway: Seeking Alpha 的分析師共識顯示，Mag 7 股票中，TSLA 的共識評級為 3.18，GOOGL 的共識評級為 4.03，MSFT 為 4.03，這些科技巨頭的分析師評級較為樂觀。
chart_commentary.action: 關注分析師對科技龍頭的看法，並與市場實際表現進行比對。

chart_orchestration.overall_summary: 本周總結：本週市場主線圍繞在 AI 產業的樂觀前景與地緣政治風險的博弈。台積電 ADR 的強勁表現帶動科技股上漲，但中東局勢的持續緊張和原油價格的走高為市場帶來不確定性。分析師共識顯示對部分科技巨頭持樂觀態度，但社群熱度顯示對特定題材的關注度正在升溫。週末觀察視角下，市場情緒在科技股的拉抬與宏觀風險之間尋求平衡，投資人需密切關注盤面變化。

chart_gap_suggestions.chart_name: 美股分析師共識圖 (Seeking Alpha)
chart_gap_suggestions.why_needed: 補上美股大型權值與科技股的分析師一致預期，讓報告不只看價格，也能看到評級與上行空間。
chart_gap_suggestions.evidence: 專案已有 Seeking Alpha service，可直接轉成圖表。

chart_gap_suggestions.why_needed: 把衝突事件數、致死數與未來風險預測可視化，讓地緣政治對市場的影響更直觀。
chart_gap_suggestions.evidence: 專案已有 ACLED service，但目前未做成可視化圖表。

chart_gap_suggestions.why_needed: 把 PTT 與 Dcard 的熱門討論、看多看空比與主題熱度做成圖，補足市場情緒面。
chart_gap_suggestions.evidence: 現有 trend_pulse 與社群抓取已能提供底層資料。

chart_gap_suggestions.why_needed: 晨報常聚焦 AI / 半導體 / 科技權值，將科技主題與新聞來源拆開呈現會更清楚。
chart_gap_suggestions.evidence: 目前科技內容散落在新聞主題與 AI 分析中，尚未獨立成圖。
